subject: Physics
q:
A stone tied to 180 cm long string at its end is making 28 revolutions in horizontal circle in every minute. The magnitude of acceleration of stone is 1936x m s-2. The value of x ______.

[Take π=227]
question:
A stone tied to 180 cm long string at its end is making 28 revolutions in horizontal circle in every minute. The magnitude of acceleration of stone is 1936x m s-2. The value of x ______.

[Take π=227]
solution:
Angular velocity, ω=28×2π60 rad s-1.

Required centripetal acceleration is given by,

a=ω2R

=5660×2272×1.8

=(44)2225×1.8=1936125

Hence, required x=125.

subject: Physics
q: A projectile is given an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, where <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mtext>i</mtext> <mo>^</mo> </mover> </mrow> </math> is along the ground and <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mtext>j</mtext> <mo>^</mo> </mover> </mrow> </math> is along the vertical upward. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>m</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the equation of its trajectory is :
question: A projectile is given an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, where <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mtext>i</mtext> <mo>^</mo> </mover> </mrow> </math> is along the ground and <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mtext>j</mtext> <mo>^</mo> </mover> </mrow> </math> is along the vertical upward. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>m</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the equation of its trajectory is :
solution:
v → = i ^ + 2 j ^

⇒ x = t    ..... (i)

y = 2 t - 1 2 1 0 t 2    ..... (ii)

From (i) and (ii)

y = 2 x - 5 x 2

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with constant speed in a circular path. When the particle turns by an angle 90°, the ratio of instantaneous velocity to its average velocity is π:x2. The value of x will be
question: A particle is moving with constant speed in a circular path. When the particle turns by an angle 90°, the ratio of instantaneous velocity to its average velocity is π:x2. The value of x will be
solution:
In circular motion with constant speed, magnitude of instantaneous velocity, vins=ωr.

Time taken to turn by 90° will be =π2ω=π2ω.

Displacement will be equal to the length of  AB=r2+r2=r2

Average velocity

vavg=displacementtime=r2×2ωπ=22πωr

Hence, required ratio vins vavg =ωrπ22ωr=π22

Therefore, x=2.

subject: Physics
q: A stone is projected at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The ratio of kinetic energy of the stone at point of projection to its kinetic energy at the highest point of flight will be :
question: A stone is projected at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The ratio of kinetic energy of the stone at point of projection to its kinetic energy at the highest point of flight will be :
solution:
Let stone is projected with initial velocity v.

Kinetic energy of the stone at point of projection is KE1=12mv2.

Now, velocity of stone at maximum height is =vcos30°, so kinetic energy at that point is KE2=12mvcos30°2

Hence, the ratio KE1KE2=12mv212mvcos30°2=43

subject: Physics
q: A particle moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with uniform speed takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> to complete one revolution. If this particle is projected with the same speed at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is then given by :
question: A particle moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with uniform speed takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> to complete one revolution. If this particle is projected with the same speed at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is then given by :
solution:
Time period of circular motion is given by, 

2πRv=T⇒v=2πRT

Now, maximum height H=v2sin2θ2g

⇒4R=4π2R2T2sin2θ2g

⇒sinθ=2gT2π2R

⇒θ=sin−12gT2π2R12

subject: Physics
q:
A gun mounted on the ground fires bullets in all directions with same speed. The farthest distance the bullets could reach is $6.4$ m. The speed of the bullets from the gun is ______ m/s.
(take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
question:
A gun mounted on the ground fires bullets in all directions with same speed. The farthest distance the bullets could reach is $6.4$ m. The speed of the bullets from the gun is ______ m/s.
(take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
The maximum horizontal range of a projectile is given by $R_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{u^2}{g}$.

Given $R_{\text{max}} = 6.4$ m and $g = 10$ m/s$^2$.

Substituting the values:

$6.4 = \dfrac{u^2}{10}$

$u^2 = 64$

$u = 8$ m/s.

Answer: $8$

subject: Physics
q: A fighter jet is flying horizontally at a certain altitude with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. When it passes directly overhead an anti-aircraft gun, a bullet is fired from the gun, at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the horizontal, to hit the jet. If the bullet speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo></math>.
question: A fighter jet is flying horizontally at a certain altitude with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. When it passes directly overhead an anti-aircraft gun, a bullet is fired from the gun, at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the horizontal, to hit the jet. If the bullet speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo></math>.
solution:
In order to hit, horizontal velocity of both moving object should be same,

V1=V2cosθ ⇒200=400cosθ

⇒cosθ=12 ⇒θ=60°

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is suspended from a ceiling through a string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. The particle moves in a horizontal circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>L</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. The speed of particle will be :
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is suspended from a ceiling through a string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. The particle moves in a horizontal circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>L</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. The speed of particle will be :
solution:
sinθ=12

θ=45°

tanθ=mv2rmg=v2rg=1

v2=rg

v=rg

subject: Physics
q: A small particle of mass $m$ is projected at an angle $\theta$ with the $\mathrm{x}$-axis with an initial velocity $\mathrm{v}_0$ in the $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}$ plane as shown in the figure. At a time $t < \frac{v_0 \sin \theta}{g}$, the angular momentum of the particle is where $\hat{\mathrm{i}}, \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ are unit vectors along $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}$ and $\mathrm{z}$-axis respectively.
question: A small particle of mass $m$ is projected at an angle $\theta$ with the $\mathrm{x}$-axis with an initial velocity $\mathrm{v}_0$ in the $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}$ plane as shown in the figure. At a time $t < \frac{v_0 \sin \theta}{g}$, the angular momentum of the particle is where $\hat{\mathrm{i}}, \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ are unit vectors along $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}$ and $\mathrm{z}$-axis respectively.
solution: $\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}} & =m(\vec{r} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}) \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}} & =m\left[\mathrm{v}_0 \cos \theta t \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{v}_0 \sin \theta \mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{2} g \mathrm{t}^2\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right] \times\left[\mathrm{v}_0 \cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{v}_0 \sin \theta-g \mathrm{~g}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right] \\ & =m \mathrm{v}_0 \cos \theta \mathrm{t}\left[-\frac{1}{2} g \mathrm{~g}\right] \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & =-\frac{1}{2} m g \mathrm{v}_0 \mathrm{t}^2 \cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A car moving with a speed of $54$ km/h takes a turn of radius $20$ m. A simple pendulum is suspended from the ceiling of the car. Determine the angle made by the string of the pendulum with the vertical during the turning. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
question: A car moving with a speed of $54$ km/h takes a turn of radius $20$ m. A simple pendulum is suspended from the ceiling of the car. Determine the angle made by the string of the pendulum with the vertical during the turning. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Speed of the car, $v = 54 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 15$ m/s.

In the frame of the car, the pendulum bob experiences a pseudo force (centrifugal force) horizontally outwards and the gravitational force downwards.

Let $T$ be the tension in the string and $\theta$ be the angle it makes with the vertical. For equilibrium in the car's frame, the horizontal and vertical forces balance:

$T \sin \theta = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

$T \cos \theta = mg$

Dividing the two equations, we get:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{v^2}{rg}$

Substituting the given values $v = 15$ m/s, $r = 20$ m, and $g = 10$ m/s$^2$:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{(15)^2}{20 \times 10} = \dfrac{225}{200} = 1.125$

$\theta = \tan^{-1}(1.125)$

Answer: $\tan^{-1}(1.125)$

subject: Physics
q: A child stands on the edge of the cliff <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground and throws a stone horizontally with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Neglecting the air resistance, the speed with which the stone hits the ground will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> (given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> ).
question: A child stands on the edge of the cliff <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground and throws a stone horizontally with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Neglecting the air resistance, the speed with which the stone hits the ground will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> (given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> ).
solution:
Given here, u=5 m s-1, h=10 m

Using vy2=uy2+2gh in vertical direction, we have the velocity of stone in vertical direction as vy=2gh=200 m s-1   (since, uy=0).

The final velocity along the horizontal direction is the same as the initial horizontal velocity as there is no acceleration along the x-axis.

The speed with which the stone hits the ground is 

vnet=vx2+vy2=25+200=15 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is thrown vertically upward. Another ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is thrown an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the vertical. Both the balls stay in air for the same period of time. The ratio of the heights attained by the two balls respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is thrown vertically upward. Another ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is thrown an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the vertical. Both the balls stay in air for the same period of time. The ratio of the heights attained by the two balls respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
When the first ball of mass m is projected vertically upwards, the second ball of mass 2m makes an angle θ with the vertical.

The time of flight is the same for both the balls.

Then, T1=T2 ⇒2u1g=2u2sinθg

⇒u1=u2sinθ    ...1

Maximum height of projectile is given by H=u2sin2θ2g

Ratio of the heights is H1H2=u12u22sin2θ    ...2

From equation 1 and 2, we have 

⇒H1H2=1.

Thus, x=1.

subject: Physics
q:
A particle of mass m is moving along a trajectory given by
x=x0+a cosω1t
y=y0+b sinω2t
The torque, acting on the particle about the origin, at t=0 is:
question:
A particle of mass m is moving along a trajectory given by
x=x0+a cosω1t
y=y0+b sinω2t
The torque, acting on the particle about the origin, at t=0 is:
solution:
x=x0+a cosω1t
y=y0+b sin ω2t
ax=-a ω12cos ω1t
ay=-b ω22sin ω2t
At t=0 ,
F→=ma→=-a ω12 i^
And
r→=x0+ a i^+y0 j^
Torque, τ=r→×F→
τ=x0+ a i^+y0 j^×-a ω12 i^
=my0 aω12 k^

subject: Physics
q: A particle just clears a wall of height $b$ at distance $a$ and strikes the ground at a distance $c$ from the point of projection. The angle of projection is
question: A particle just clears a wall of height $b$ at distance $a$ and strikes the ground at a distance $c$ from the point of projection. The angle of projection is
solution: $a=(u \cos \alpha) t$ and $b=(u \sin \alpha) t-\frac{1}{2} g t^2$ $b=a \tan \alpha-\frac{1}{2} g \frac{a^2}{u^2 \cos ^2 \alpha}$ also, $c=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \alpha}{g}$ $b=a \tan \alpha-\frac{a^2 g}{2}\left(\frac{\sin 2 \alpha}{c g}\right) \sec ^2 \alpha$ $b=a \tan \alpha-\frac{a^2}{2 c} 2 \tan \alpha$ $\Rightarrow\left(a-\frac{a^2}{c}\right) \tan \alpha=b$ $\tan \alpha=\frac{b c}{a(c-a)}$.

subject: Physics
q: A bomb is dropped by a fighter plane flying horizontally. To an observer sitting in the plane, the trajectory of the bomb is a :
question: A bomb is dropped by a fighter plane flying horizontally. To an observer sitting in the plane, the trajectory of the bomb is a :
options: straight line vertically down the plane
parabola in a direction opposite to the motion of plane
parabola in the direction of motion of plane
solution: The bomb after being dropped from the plane will have only horizontal velocity but zero vertical velocity. This scenario is as same as if a body is horizontally projected from the top of a tower, and it hits the ground tracing a parabolic path. Hence, the trajectory of the bomb is also a parabola, in the direction of motion of the plane. But if we look the trajectory with respect to observer sitting in the plane then this looks like straight line vertically down the plane due to same horizontal velocity.

subject: Physics
q: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
question: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
solution:
At A: $KE_A = K$, $v_A = v$, angle $= 60°$.
At B (highest point): $v_B = v\cos 60° = v/2$, so $KE_B = K/4$.
At C (same level as A): by energy conservation, $KE_C = K$.
$|KE_B - KE_C| = |K/4 - K| = 3K/4$.
Ratio $= \frac{3K/4}{K} = \frac{3}{4}$, i.e., $3:4$.

subject: Physics
q: A projectile is projected with velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the horizontal. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> seconds its inclination with horizontal becomes zero. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> represents horizontal range of the projectile, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> will be : [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>use</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A projectile is projected with velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with the horizontal. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> seconds its inclination with horizontal becomes zero. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> represents horizontal range of the projectile, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> will be : [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>use</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
From the information given in the question, after time t the inclination of the projectile becomes zero i.e. the projectile is at the highest point. Therefore,

t=T2=usinθg.

The rang of the projectile R=2u2sinθcosθg.

Now,

Rt2=2u2sinθcosθg×g2u2sin2θ=2gcotθ⇒θ=cot-1R20t2

subject: Physics
q:
A particle is projected at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ from horizontal at a speed of $60 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The height traversed by the particle in the first second is $\mathrm{h}_0$ and height traversed in the last second, before it reaches the maximum height, is $h_1$. The ratio $h_0: h_1$ is _________
[Take, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ]
question:
A particle is projected at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ from horizontal at a speed of $60 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The height traversed by the particle in the first second is $\mathrm{h}_0$ and height traversed in the last second, before it reaches the maximum height, is $h_1$. The ratio $h_0: h_1$ is _________
[Take, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{S}_1=30 \times 1-\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 1=25 \\ & \mathrm{~S}_3=30+\left(\frac{-10}{2}\right) \times(2 \times 3-1)=5 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~S}_1}{\mathrm{~S}_3}=\frac{25}{5}=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A vehicle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving along a levelled curved road of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The centripetal force acting on the vehicle is:
question: A vehicle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving along a levelled curved road of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The centripetal force acting on the vehicle is:
solution:
The centripetal force is given by 

F=Mv2R=MRω2

The given data is 

M=200 kgR=70 mω=0.2 rad s-1

The magnitude of the force is 

F=200×70×0.22 N

⇒F=560 N

subject: Physics
q:
A projectile fired at 30° to the ground is observed to be at same height at time 3 s and 5 s after projection, during its flight. The speed of projection of the projectile is m s-1.
(Given g=10 m s-2)
question:
A projectile fired at 30° to the ground is observed to be at same height at time 3 s and 5 s after projection, during its flight. The speed of projection of the projectile is m s-1.
(Given g=10 m s-2)
solution:
The time taken to reach maximum height is 

T'=usinθg.

The total time of flight is T=(3+5) s=8 s.

Hence,

T'=T2=3+52=usinθg

⇒4=(u)×1210

⇒u=80 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: An object moves at a constant speed along a circular path in a horizontal plane with centre at the origin. When the object is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The object’s velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>v</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be
question: An object moves at a constant speed along a circular path in a horizontal plane with centre at the origin. When the object is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The object’s velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>v</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be
solution: As the speed is constant, only centripetal acceleration will act(towards the centre of the circular path), ac=v2r=422=8 m s-2 towards the centre which at the given time will be towards right, hence a→c=8i^ m s-2. Velocity of the particle at this instant will in upward direction and hence v→=4j^ m s-1.

subject: Physics
q:
Two particles A, B are moving on two concentric circles of radii R1 and R2 with equal angular speed ω. At t=0, their positions and direction of motion are shown in the figure:

The relative velocity VA→-VB→ at t=π2ω is given by:
question:
Two particles A, B are moving on two concentric circles of radii R1 and R2 with equal angular speed ω. At t=0, their positions and direction of motion are shown in the figure:

The relative velocity VA→-VB→ at t=π2ω is given by:
solution:
t=π2ω

V→A=ωR1-i^

V→B=ωR2-i^

VA→-VB→=ωR2-R1i^
